lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
As to our translations of the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, yours was doing harm, and mine was necessary in self-defence. You should be glad that heresy is exposed.

title: As to our translations of the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, yours was doing harm, and mine was necessary in self-defence. You should be glad that heresy is exposed.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In the matter of the books Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, I have even a claim upon your gratitude.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that you cut off anything that was offensive and replaced it by what was better.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have represented things just as they stood in the Greek.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: By this means both things are made to appear, your faith and the heresy of him whom you translated.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The leading Christians of Rome wrote to me: Answer your accuser; if you keep silence, you will be held to have assented to his charges.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: All of them unanimously demanded that I should bring to light the subtle errors of Origen, and make known the poison of the heretics to the ears of the Romans to put them on their guard.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How can this be an injury to you?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Have you a monopoly of the translation of these books?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are there no others who take part in this work?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When you translated parts of the Septuagint, did you mean to prohibit all others from translating it after your version had been published?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why, I also have translated many books from the Greek.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You have full power to make a second translation of them at your pleasure; for both the good and the bad in them must be laid to the charge of their author.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And this would hold in your case also, had you not said that you had cut out the heretical parts and translated only what was positively good.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This is a difficulty which you have made for yourself, and which cannot be solved, except by confessing that you have erred as all men err, and condemning your former opinion.
